title: Insert word in Trie
description: Implement the insert operation for a Trie (Prefix Tree) data structure. All inputs are lowercase English letters unless specified otherwise.

examples.explanation: 1. Start at root node (is_end=False, children all None). 2. Process 'c': index = ord('c')-ord('a')=2. root.children[2] is None, create new TrieNode. Move to node for 'c'. 3. Process 'a': index=0. current.children[0] is None, create new TrieNode. Move to node for 'a'. 4. Process 't': index=19. current.children[19] is None, create new TrieNode. Move to node for 't'. 5. Set current.is_end = True. Result: root->'c'->'a'->'t' (with is_end=True at 't').

1. Start at root. 2. Process 'c': index=2. root.children[2] exists (node for 'c'), move to it. 3. Process 'a': index=0. current.children[0] exists (node for 'a'), move to it. 4. Process 'r': index=17. current.children[17] is None, create new TrieNode. Move to node for 'r'. 5. Set current.is_end = True. Result: root->'c'->'a'->'t' (is_end=True) and root->'c'->'a'->'r' (is_end=True).

approaches.name: Approach 1: Standard Trie with Array Children (26)
approaches.summary: Insert word using fixed-size array of 26 children nodes (for lowercase English letters). Fast child access with O(1) lookup.

ladders.title: L0: Full Problem — Insert Word using Array-based Trie
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of inserting a word into a Trie data structure using fixed-size array child nodes.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires implementing a complete Trie insertion function that handles all edge cases. The intuition is that a Trie stores words character by character in a tree structure where each node represents a character and has children for possible next characters. We start at the root node and traverse/insert nodes for each character in the word, marking the end node to indicate a complete word. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize current node as root. 2. For each character in the word: a. Calculate index (0-25 for lowercase English letters). b. If child at index is null, create new TrieNode. c. Move current node to child at index. 3. Mark current node as end of word. Time complexity is O(L) where L is the length of the word, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(n * 26) worst-case for new nodes, but O(n) in practice since we only create nodes for characters in the word. This connects to the overall data structure by implementing the core insertion operation. Common mistakes include: not handling empty strings properly, forgetting to mark end of word, incorrect index calculation for non-lowercase letters, and not creating new nodes when needed. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = [None] * 26; self.is_end = False. Insert method: def insert(word): current = root; for char in word: index = ord(char) - ord('a'); if not current.children[index]: current.children[index] = TrieNode(); current = current.children[index]; current.is_end = True.

examples.trace: 1. Start at root node (is_end=False, children all None). 2. Process 'c': index = ord('c')-ord('a')=2. root.children[2] is None, create new TrieNode. Move to node for 'c'. 3. Process 'a': index=0. current.children[0] is None, create new TrieNode. Move to node for 'a'. 4. Process 't': index=19. current.children[19] is None, create new TrieNode. Move to node for 't'. 5. Set current.is_end = True. Result: root->'c'->'a'->'t' (with is_end=True at 't').

1. Start at root. 2. Process 'c': index=2. root.children[2] exists (node for 'c'), move to it. 3. Process 'a': index=0. current.children[0] exists (node for 'a'), move to it. 4. Process 'r': index=17. current.children[17] is None, create new TrieNode. Move to node for 'r'. 5. Set current.is_end = True. Result: root->'c'->'a'->'t' (is_end=True) and root->'c'->'a'->'r' (is_end=True).

testCases.expected: Root marked as end of word (if allowed) or no change
Both words inserted, 'app' node has is_end=True
Trie unchanged after second insert (duplicate handling)
Both words inserted, 's' node has is_end=True
All words inserted correctly with shared prefix

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traverse and Create Nodes for Word Characters
ladders.desc: The core traversal logic that moves through existing nodes and creates new ones as needed.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the main loop of insertion: iterating through each character of the word, navigating the Trie structure, and creating nodes when they don't exist. The intuition is that we need to follow the path of characters in the Trie, building it as we go if necessary. Step-by-step: 1. Start with current node as input. 2. For each character: a. Calculate array index. b. Check if child exists at that index. c. If not, create new node. d. Move to child node. 3. Return the final node. Time: O(n) for word length n. Space: O(1) extra space. This feeds into L0 by providing the node traversal that precedes marking the end of word. Common mistakes: incorrect index bounds, not updating current node properly, creating nodes unnecessarily when they exist. Real pattern: def traverse_and_create(word, start_node): current = start_node; for char in word: idx = ord(char)-97; if not current.children[idx]: current.children[idx] = TrieNode(); current = current.children[idx]; return current.

examples.trace: 1. current = root. 2. char 'd': idx=3, root.children[3] is None, create node, current = node_d. 3. char 'o': idx=14, node_d.children[14] is None, create node, current = node_o. 4. char 'g': idx=6, node_o.children[6] is None, create node, current = node_g. Return node_g.

examples.input: Word 'at', start at node after 'c' (from existing 'cat')
examples.trace: 1. current = node_c (from existing). 2. char 'a': idx=0, node_c.children[0] exists (from 'cat'), current = node_a. 3. char 't': idx=19, node_a.children[19] exists (from 'cat'), current = node_t. Return node_t (no new nodes created).

testCases.expected: Returns node for 'i' after creating 2 new nodes
Returns node for 'x' after creating 1 node
Returns node for 'b', creating only 'b' node

testCases.input: Word 'test', start at root with 'te' already existing
testCases.expected: Returns node for 't', creating 's' and final 't' nodes
Returns node for second 'z' after creating 2 nodes

testCases.input: Word 'cat', start at root with 'car' existing
testCases.expected: Returns node for 't', creating 't' node only
Returns node for 'm' after creating 9 nodes
Returns node for 'a' after creating 1 node

testCases.input: Word 'same', start at root with 'same' existing
testCases.expected: Returns existing node for 'e' (no new nodes)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Character to Array Index Mapping with Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Converting characters to array indices and handling edge cases for valid Trie storage.
ladders.explanation: This core logic handles the fundamental mapping between characters and array indices in the Trie node. The intuition is that we need a consistent way to store 26 possible lowercase letters in a fixed-size array. We map 'a' to index 0, 'b' to 1, ..., 'z' to 25 using ASCII arithmetic. Step-by-step: 1. Take input character. 2. Validate it's lowercase English letter (optional but important). 3. Calculate index = ord(char) - ord('a'). 4. Ensure index is between 0 and 25 inclusive. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the index calculation needed for array access. Common mistakes: not handling uppercase or special characters, off-by-one errors in index calculation, assuming ASCII without validation. Real pattern: def char_to_index(char): if not 'a' <= char <= 'z': raise ValueError; return ord(char) - 97.

ladders.title: Building Block — Creating and Initializing a Trie Node
ladders.desc: Constructing a single Trie node with array for children and end-of-word flag.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating the fundamental unit of the Trie data structure. The intuition is that each node must store two things: an array of references to possible next characters (26 for lowercase English), and a boolean flag indicating if this node completes a word. Step-by-step: 1. Define a class/structure for TrieNode. 2. Initialize an array of 26 elements, all set to null/None. 3. Initialize is_end boolean to false. Time: O(1) for creation. Space: O(26) for the array. This connects to L2 by providing the node structure that uses the character indices. Common mistakes: incorrect array size, not initializing all elements to null, forgetting the end flag. Real pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = [None] * 26; self.is_end = False.

examples.trace: 1. Allocate memory for node. 2. Create array of size 26. 3. Set each element to None. 4. Set is_end = False. Result: node.children[0..25] = None, node.is_end = False.
1. Create node as above. 2. Set node.is_end = True. Result: children all None, is_end=True.

testCases.input: Create node and set is_end=True, check is_end
Create node and verify children array type
Create node and modify children[5] to point to another node
Create node and check initial is_end value
Create node and verify all children are None

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Modifying Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Fundamental array operations: checking if element exists and creating new element at index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves working with arrays at a fundamental level: accessing elements by index and modifying them. The intuition is that we need to check if a child node exists at a specific index (null check) and optionally create a new node there. Step-by-step: 1. Given array and index. 2. Check if array[index] is null. 3. If null and we need to create: allocate new node, assign to array[index]. 4. Return array[index]. Time: O(1). Space: O(1) or O(size of node) if creating. This connects to L3 by showing how to use the children array in a TrieNode. Common mistakes: index out of bounds, not handling null properly, incorrect assignment. Real pattern: def access_or_create(arr, idx): if arr[idx] is None: arr[idx] = TrieNode(); return arr[idx].

examples.input: Array [None, None, None], index 1, create new node
examples.trace: 1. arr = [None, None, None], idx=1. 2. arr[1] is None. 3. Create new TrieNode. 4. arr[1] = new_node. Result: [None, node, None].

examples.input: Array [None, existing_node, None], index 1, no create
examples.trace: 1. arr = [None, node_x, None], idx=1. 2. arr[1] is not None. 3. Return node_x. Array unchanged.

testCases.input: Array of 3 None, index 0, create node
Array [nodeA, None, nodeC], index 1, create node
Array all None, index 2, no create (just check)
Array with node at index 5, index 5, access
Array with nodes at indices 0,1,2, index 1, access
Array single element None, index 0, create node
Array with node at index 10, index 10, create node (duplicate)
testCases.expected: Return existing node, no new creation

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Fixed-Size Arrays and Null References
ladders.desc: Understanding how arrays store references and how null/None represents missing children.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basics of arrays and null references. The intuition is that an array is a contiguous block of elements accessible by index, and null represents the absence of a value. In our Trie, we use an array of 26 slots (for a-z), where each slot can either contain a reference to a child node or be null if that character doesn't continue from this node. Step-by-step: 1. Array declaration: create fixed-size collection. 2. Initialization: set all elements to null. 3. Access: use index to read/write elements. 4. Null check: determine if element is set or empty. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) for array size. This connects to L4 by providing the basic data structure used. Common mistakes: confusing index numbering (0-based), not initializing arrays, misunderstanding null vs default values. Real pattern: arr = [None] * size; if arr[i] is None: print('empty'); else: print('has value').

examples.input: Create array of size 3 with nulls
examples.trace: 1. size = 3. 2. Allocate memory for 3 elements. 3. Set each element to None. Result: array where arr[0]=None, arr[1]=None, arr[2]=None.
1. arr = [None, 5, None]. 2. Access arr[1]. 3. Value is 5, which is not None. Result: element exists with value 5.

testCases.input: Array with mixed null and values, check null slot
Array size 2, set index 0 to 5, check

approaches.name: Approach 2: Trie with HashMap/Dictionary Children
approaches.summary: Insert word using hashmap (dictionary) for children nodes. More memory-efficient for sparse or variable character sets.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Insert Word into Trie Using HashMap Nodes
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of inserting a word into a Trie where each node uses a hashmap to store children.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires inserting a word into a Trie data structure where each node contains a hashmap mapping characters to child nodes, plus a boolean flag marking word endings. The intuition is that a Trie efficiently stores strings with shared prefixes, and using hashmaps at nodes allows dynamic creation of children without pre-allocating fixed arrays, making it memory-efficient for sparse character distributions (like Unicode) and variable character sets. The algorithm starts at the root node, iterates through each character of the word, and for each character: checks if it exists as a key in the current node's children hashmap; if not, creates a new TrieNode with an empty hashmap and adds it; then moves to that child node. After processing all characters, marks the last node's end-of-word flag as true. Time complexity is O(n) where n is word length, as each character requires O(1) hashmap operations. Space complexity is O(n) for storing new nodes in the worst case (no shared prefixes). This connects to the broader use of Tries for autocomplete, spell-checking, and IP routing. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark the end-of-word flag (leading to prefix confusion), not handling empty strings properly, or incorrectly updating the current node pointer. Real code pattern: class TrieNode with children dict and is_end flag; insert method loops through word chars, gets or creates nodes, sets is_end.

examples.trace: 1. Start at root node (children: {}). 2. Char 'c': not in root's children, create new TrieNode (children: {}), add to root's children {'c': node_c}. Move to node_c. 3. Char 'a': not in node_c's children, create new TrieNode (children: {}), add to node_c's children {'a': node_a}. Move to node_a. 4. Char 't': not in node_a's children, create new TrieNode (children: {}), add to node_a's children {'t': node_t}. Move to node_t. 5. Set node_t.is_end = True. Result: root -> 'c' -> 'a' -> 't' (is_end=True).

1. Start at root node (children: {'c': node_c}). 2. Char 'c': in root's children, move to node_c (children: {'a': node_a}). 3. Char 'a': in node_c's children, move to node_a (children: {'t': node_t}). 4. Char 'r': not in node_a's children, create new TrieNode (children: {}), add to node_a's children {'t': node_t, 'r': node_r}. Move to node_r. 5. Set node_r.is_end = True. Result: root -> 'c' -> 'a' -> 't' (is_end=True) and 'r' (is_end=True).

testCases.expected: Trie with empty string inserted, root.is_end=True

testCases.input: Insert 'a' into empty Trie, then insert 'a' again
testCases.expected: Trie with 'a' inserted once, root->'a' (is_end=True), duplicate insert does nothing structural

testCases.input: Insert 'test' into empty Trie, then insert 'testing'
Insert 'apple' into empty Trie, then insert 'app'
Insert 'zoo' into empty Trie, then insert 'zoom'

testCases.expected: Trie with '123' inserted, handles digits via hashmap
Trie with 'café' inserted, handles Unicode via hashmap

testCases.input: Insert 'a', then 'ab', then 'abc' into empty Trie
testCases.expected: Trie with all three, shared prefixes

testCases.input: Insert 'cat' into empty Trie, verify by checking 'cat' exists

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traverse or Create Nodes for Word Insertion
ladders.desc: The core function that traverses the Trie for a word, creating new nodes as needed.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the insertion process: given a root TrieNode and a word, it traverses down the Trie following the characters, creating new nodes whenever a character is missing in the current node's children hashmap. The intuition is that to insert a word, we need to ensure a path exists for all its characters, building it dynamically if not present. The algorithm: initialize current node as root; for each character in the word: check if character is a key in current node's children hashmap; if not, create a new TrieNode with empty children hashmap and add it to current node's children with the character as key; then update current node to this child node. After the loop, return the final current node (which will be marked as end-of-word in the full insertion). Time complexity is O(n) for the word length, with O(1) hashmap operations per character. Space complexity is O(1) extra space, but may allocate O(n) new nodes if none exist. This feeds into L0 by handling the node traversal and creation, leaving just the end-of-word marking. Common mistakes include not updating the current node pointer correctly (leading to infinite loops), or assuming the word is non-empty without checks. Real code pattern: def traverse_or_create(root, word): current = root; for char in word: if char not in current.children: current.children[char] = TrieNode(); current = current.children[char]; return current.

examples.trace: 1. Current = root (children: {}). 2. Char 'd': not in children, create new node_d, add to root's children {'d': node_d}, current = node_d. 3. Char 'o': not in node_d's children, create new node_o, add to node_d's children {'o': node_o}, current = node_o. 4. Char 'g': not in node_o's children, create new node_g, add to node_o's children {'g': node_g}, current = node_g. Return node_g.
1. Current = root (children: {'d': node_d}). 2. Char 'd': in children, current = node_d (children: {'o': node_o}). 3. Char 'o': in node_d's children, current = node_o. Return node_o.

testCases.expected: Node after 'i', with new nodes for 'h' and 'i'

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dynamic Node Creation with Hashmaps
ladders.desc: The central technique of using a hashmap at each node to store children and creating nodes on-demand.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves representing each Trie node with a hashmap (dictionary) that maps characters to child TrieNodes, and dynamically creating new child nodes when inserting a character that isn't already a key. The intuition is that instead of pre-allocating a fixed-size array for all possible characters (e.g., 26 for lowercase English), which wastes memory for sparse tries or large character sets, we use a hashmap to store only existing children, making it efficient for variable and Unicode character sets. The technique: each TrieNode has a 'children' hashmap; when inserting, for a given character at a current node, check if the character is in the hashmap keys; if not, instantiate a new TrieNode, add it to the hashmap with the character as key. This allows the Trie to grow organically as words are added. Time complexity per insertion step is O(1) average for hashmap operations. Space complexity is proportional to the number of distinct characters stored across nodes. This feeds into L1 by providing the mechanism for the traverse-or-create sub-routine. Common mistakes include using a list instead of hashmap (leading to O(n) lookups), or not initializing the hashmap properly. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; then in insertion: if char not in node.children: node.children[char] = TrieNode().

examples.trace: 1. Node children hashmap is empty: {}. 2. Character 'b' is not a key in the hashmap. 3. Create new TrieNode with empty children hashmap. 4. Add key 'b' with value new_node to hashmap: {'b': new_node}.

examples.input: Insert 'a' at node with children {'b': node_b}
examples.output: Node now has children {'b': node_b, 'a': new_node}
examples.trace: 1. Node children hashmap: {'b': node_b}. 2. Character 'a' is not a key. 3. Create new TrieNode. 4. Add key 'a' with value new_node: {'b': node_b, 'a': new_node}.

testCases.input: Node children {'m': node_m}, insert 'm' multiple times

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Node Structure with Hashmap and End Flag
ladders.desc: Defining the Trie node that contains a hashmap for children and a boolean for word endings.
ladders.explanation: This building block is the fundamental unit of the Trie: a node that stores its child nodes in a hashmap and has a boolean flag to indicate if it represents the end of a word. The intuition is that a Trie is a tree where each node corresponds to a prefix of stored words; the hashmap allows efficient lookup and insertion of child nodes by character, and the end flag distinguishes between prefixes that are complete words (e.g., 'car' in a Trie with 'car' and 'cart'). The structure: each TrieNode has two attributes: 'children' (a hashmap/dictionary mapping characters to TrieNode references) and 'is_end' (a boolean, initially False). This enables the Trie to support operations like insertion, search, and prefix checking. Time and space analysis for the node itself is O(1) for initialization, but the children hashmap can grow. This feeds into L2 by providing the node type used in dynamic creation. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize 'is_end' as False, or using a list for children without hashmap benefits. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False.

examples.trace: 1. Instantiate TrieNode object. 2. Initialize children as empty dictionary: {}. 3. Initialize is_end as boolean False.

examples.output: Node representing end of a word with one child
examples.trace: 1. Node has children hashmap with one key 'a' mapping to another TrieNode. 2. is_end is True, meaning this node marks the end of a stored word (e.g., if word is 'a').

testCases.expected: Valid node for empty string or word end

testCases.input: Node with children={'x': node_x, 'y': node_y}, is_end=True
testCases.expected: Node with multiple children and word end

testCases.input: Set is_end to True on a node
Add child 'c' to node with children {}

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Hashmap Lookup and Insertion by Key
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of checking if a key exists in a hashmap and inserting if not.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a hashmap (dictionary) to check for the existence of a key and, if absent, inserting a new key-value pair. The intuition is that in the Trie insertion, for each character, we need to see if it's already a child of the current node (via the hashmap key) and if not, add it with a new node as value. The operation: given a hashmap 'map' and a key 'k', check if k in map; if not, map[k] = v (where v is the new value). This is O(1) average time due to hashmap properties. Space may increase by one key-value pair. This feeds into L3 as the mechanism for managing children in the TrieNode. Common mistakes include using map.get(k) without handling None, or inserting without checking existence first (which may overwrite). Real code pattern: if key not in hashmap: hashmap[key] = value.

examples.trace: 1. Map is empty: {}. 2. Check if 'a' is a key: not present. 3. Insert key 'a' with value 1: {'a': 1}.
1. Map: {'b': 2}. 2. Check if 'b' is a key: present. 3. Do nothing, map remains {'b': 2}.

testCases.input: Map {'m': node_m, 'n': node_n}, key 'o', value node_o

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — HashMap/Dictionary as Dynamic Mapping
ladders.desc: Understanding how dictionaries store key-value pairs for flexible child lookup.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces hashmaps (dictionaries in Python), which are data structures that store key-value pairs and allow fast lookup, insertion, and deletion based on keys. The intuition is that a hashmap uses a hash function to map keys to indices in an underlying array, providing average O(1) time for operations, making it efficient for scenarios where you need to associate data (like characters to child nodes in a Trie). Key operations: initialization (empty hashmap), checking if a key exists (using 'in' operator), getting a value by key, setting a value for a key (inserts or updates). For example, in Python: map = {} creates an empty dict; 'a' in map checks existence; map['a'] = 1 inserts or updates. Time complexity is O(1) average for these operations, but can degrade to O(n) in worst-case collisions. Space complexity is O(n) for n key-value pairs. This feeds into L4 as the basis for the lookup-and-insert operation. Common mistakes include assuming order (hashmaps are unordered in standard versions), or not handling missing keys properly. Real code pattern: map = {}; map[key] = value; if key in map: ...

examples.input: Create hashmap, add key 'name' with value 'Alice'
examples.trace: 1. Initialize empty hashmap: {}. 2. Assign map['name'] = 'Alice'. 3. Hashmap now contains key 'name' mapping to value 'Alice'.

examples.input: Map {'age': 25}, check if 'age' exists, then add 'city' with 'NYC'
examples.trace: 1. Initial map: {'age': 25}. 2. Check 'age' in map: True. 3. Add map['city'] = 'NYC'. 4. Map becomes {'age': 25, 'city': 'NYC'}.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Compressed Trie (Patricia Trie) Insertion
approaches.summary: Insert word into a Compressed Trie by merging single-child paths and splitting nodes when needed. Optimizes space for long common prefixes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Insert Word into Compressed Trie
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of inserting a word into a compressed Trie (Patricia Trie) with node compression.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires implementing word insertion into a compressed Trie, which optimizes space by merging nodes with single children. The intuition is that standard Tries can have long chains of single-child nodes, wasting space and increasing traversal time. By compressing these chains into single nodes containing substrings, we reduce depth and improve efficiency for datasets with common prefixes. The algorithm starts with an empty root node. For insertion, we traverse the Trie, comparing characters of the word with existing node labels. If a node matches partially, we split it; if fully, we continue; if no match, we add a new child. After insertion, we check for compression opportunities by merging nodes with single children. Step-by-step: 1. Start at root. 2. For each character in word, find matching child node based on prefix. 3. If match found: a. If label fully matches word segment, move to child and continue. b. If partial match, split node: create new intermediate node with common prefix, adjust children. 4. If no match, create new leaf node with remaining word. 5. After insertion, traverse up from insertion point to compress single-child chains by merging labels. Time complexity is O(n) where n is word length, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(n) for storing nodes and labels. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of node splitting and compression. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty words or duplicate insertions, and incorrect splitting logic. Real code pattern: class CompressedTrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False. Insert function takes word, traverses, splits/creates nodes, and compresses.

examples.trace: 1. Start at root (empty). 2. No children match 'c', create new child node with label 'cat', set is_end=True. 3. No compression needed (single node). Result: root has one child 'cat'.

examples.output: Trie: root -> node('ca', is_end=False) with children 't' (is_end=True) and 'r' (is_end=True)
examples.trace: 1. Start at root, child 'cat' matches 'ca' partially. 2. Split 'cat' into 'ca' (parent) and 't' (child). 3. Add new child 'r' under 'ca'. 4. Set is_end=True for 't' and 'r'. 5. Compress: 'ca' has two children, no merge. Result: compressed structure.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Split and Merge Nodes in Compressed Trie
ladders.desc: Implement node splitting for partial matches and merging for compression in a compressed Trie.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the core operations of splitting a node when a word insertion partially matches an existing label, and merging nodes to compress single-child chains. The intuition is that compression optimizes the Trie by reducing depth, and splitting ensures correct insertion without losing data. For splitting: when inserting a word and encountering a node with label L, if the word prefix matches first k characters of L (k < len(L)), we split L into two parts: common prefix (first k chars) and suffix (remaining chars). Create a new parent node with the common prefix, and move the original node's children to a new child node with the suffix. Then, add the new word's remaining part as another child. For merging: after insertion, traverse up from the new node; if a node has exactly one child and is not an end-of-word marker, merge it with its child by concatenating labels and moving children up. Step-by-step splitting: 1. Identify match length k. 2. Create new parent node with label L[:k]. 3. Create child node with label L[k:], copy original children and is_end. 4. Add new word node with remaining characters. Merging: 1. Check if current node has one child and not is_end. 2. Concatenate current label with child's label. 3. Move child's children to current node. 4. Delete child node. Time complexity: O(m) for splitting/merging where m is label length. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called during insertion to maintain compression. Common mistakes: incorrect label updates during merge, or not preserving is_end flags. Real code pattern: def split_node(node, match_len, new_label): parent = Node(label=node.label[:match_len]); child = Node(label=node.label[match_len:], children=node.children, is_end=node.is_end); parent.children[child.label[0]] = child; return parent. def merge_node(node): if len(node.children)==1 and not node.is_end: child = list(node.children.values())[0]; node.label += child.label; node.children = child.children; node.is_end = child.is_end.

examples.input: Split node label 'cat' with match 'ca' for new word 'car'
examples.output: New structure: parent 'ca' with children 't' and 'r'
examples.trace: 1. Node label 'cat', match length 2 ('ca'). 2. Create parent 'ca'. 3. Create child 't' from original 'cat'. 4. Add new child 'r' for 'car'. Result: 'ca' node with two children.

examples.input: Merge node 'a' with single child 'b' (not is_end)
examples.trace: 1. Node 'a' has one child 'b', is_end=False. 2. Concatenate labels to 'ab'. 3. Move child 'b's children (none) to 'a'. 4. Delete child 'b'. Result: single node 'ab'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Traversal and Prefix Matching in Compressed Trie
ladders.desc: Implement traversal logic to find the insertion point in a compressed Trie using prefix matching.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves traversing the compressed Trie from the root to find where a new word should be inserted, by matching prefixes of the word with node labels. The intuition is that compressed Tries store substrings in nodes, so insertion requires comparing word segments against these labels to determine matches, splits, or new branches. The algorithm: start at root with the word. For each step, check children of current node for a child whose label starts with the next character of the word. If found, compute the longest common prefix between the word's remaining part and the child's label. Cases: 1. Full match: if child's label equals the word's remaining part, move to child and mark as end if needed. 2. Partial match: if common prefix is shorter than both, split is needed (handled in L1). 3. No match: create new child with the remaining word. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize current node as root, index i=0 for word. 2. While i < len(word): a. Get next char c = word[i]. b. If current node has child with label starting with c, find child. c. Compare child label L with word[i:]; compute match length. d. Based on match, proceed as above. 3. After traversal, handle insertion and compression. Time complexity: O(n) for traversing word. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L1: This traversal identifies where to call split/merge routines. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in index updates, or incorrect match length calculation. Real code pattern: def find_insertion_point(root, word): node = root; i = 0; while i < len(word): c = word[i]; if c in node.children: child = node.children[c]; match_len = common_prefix(child.label, word[i:]); if match_len == len(child.label): i += match_len; node = child; else: return (node, child, match_len, i); else: return (node, None, 0, i); return (node, None, -1, i) for full match.

examples.input: Traverse to insert 'car' in Trie with root->'cat'
examples.output: Match at root: child 'cat' matches 'ca' partially
examples.trace: 1. Start at root, word 'car', i=0. 2. Child 'c' exists with label 'cat'. 3. Compare 'cat' with 'car': common prefix 'ca', length 2. 4. Since 2 < len('cat')=3, partial match. Return info for splitting.

examples.input: Traverse to insert 'do' in empty Trie
examples.trace: 1. Start at root, word 'do', i=0. 2. No child with 'd'. 3. Return info for creating new child.

testCases.expected: Full match of 'app' with prefix of 'apple'
Full match of 'hell' with prefix of 'hello'
Full match of 'a', then no child for 'b'
Full match of 'xy', then no child for 'z'
Full match of 'comp' with prefix of 'computer'

ladders.title: Building Block — Node Structure and Operations in Compressed Trie
ladders.desc: Define the node structure for a compressed Trie and basic operations like child management.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves designing the node class for a compressed Trie and implementing fundamental operations such as adding children, finding children by prefix, and updating labels. The intuition is that compressed Trie nodes differ from standard Trie nodes by storing substrings (labels) instead of single characters, requiring tailored data structures. A node typically has: a string label (the substring it represents), a dictionary of children (keyed by the first character of child labels), and a boolean is_end to mark word endings. Basic operations include: 1. Adding a child: insert a new node into children dict with key as first char of its label. 2. Finding a child: given a character, check if any child's label starts with that char. 3. Updating label: modify the node's label, e.g., during merge. 4. Removing a child: delete from dict. Step-by-step for adding: def add_child(node, label): key = label[0]; node.children[key] = Node(label=label). For finding: def find_child(node, char): for child in node.children.values(): if child.label.startswith(char): return child; return None. Time complexity: O(1) for add/remove, O(k) for find where k is number of children (typically small). Space: O(1) per node. Connection to L2: This node structure is used in traversal logic. Common mistakes: using single char keys without handling multi-char labels, or incorrect is_end handling. Real code pattern: class CompressedTrieNode: def __init__(self, label=''): self.label = label; self.children = {}; self.is_end = False. Methods as described.

examples.input: Create node with label 'ca' and add child 't'
examples.trace: 1. Create node with label 'ca'. 2. Create child node with label 't'. 3. Add to children with key 't'. Result: node.children = {'t': child_node}.

examples.input: Find child for char 'a' in node with children 'ab' and 'ac'
examples.trace: 1. Node has children with labels 'ab' and 'ac'. 2. Check 'ab'.startswith('a'): true, return 'ab'. Result: found child.

testCases.expected: Node with label 'hello', empty children, is_end=False

testCases.input: Find child for 'b' in node with children 'bat', 'ball'
Remove child 'x' from node with children 'x'->'xyz'
Add child 'app' to node with label '' (root)
Find child for 'c' in node with no children
Find child for 'd' in node with children 'dog', 'dot'

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Prefix Comparison and Manipulation
ladders.desc: Implement functions to compare prefixes of strings and manipulate substrings for Trie operations.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves utility functions for working with strings, specifically computing the longest common prefix between two strings and extracting substrings, which are essential for compressed Trie insertion. The intuition is that compressed Tries rely on prefix matching to determine how words relate to node labels, requiring efficient string operations. Key functions: 1. Longest common prefix (LCP): given two strings, return the length of their shared prefix. Algorithm: iterate through characters simultaneously until mismatch or end. 2. Substring extraction: get parts of strings based on indices, e.g., word[i:] for remaining portion. 3. String concatenation: combine labels during node merging. Step-by-step for LCP: def common_prefix(s1, s2): i = 0; while i < min(len(s1), len(s2)) and s1[i] == s2[i]: i += 1; return i. For substring: use slicing, e.g., s[start:end]. Time complexity: O(min(len(s1), len(s2))) for LCP, O(1) for slicing in Python. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L3: These operations are used in node structure methods. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loops, or not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: As above; ensure robust handling of edge cases.

examples.trace: 1. Compare 'h' vs 'h': match. 2. 'e' vs 'e': match. 3. 'l' vs 'a': mismatch. Stop. Result: length 2.

examples.input: Extract substring from 'computer' starting at index 3
examples.trace: 1. String 'computer', index 3. 2. Slice [3:]: characters from position 3 to end. Result: 'puter'.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Why Compression Helps in Tries
ladders.desc: Understanding the problem of long single-child chains in standard Tries and how compression reduces depth.
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces the Trie (prefix tree) data structure, a tree-like collection used to store strings where each node represents a character, and paths from root to nodes spell out words. The intuition is that Tries optimize prefix-based operations like search and insertion by sharing common prefixes among words, reducing redundancy. In a basic Trie, each node has: an array or dictionary of children for possible next characters, and a boolean flag to mark the end of a word. Operations include: 1. Insertion: traverse characters, creating nodes as needed, mark end. 2. Search: traverse characters, check if path exists and end flag is true. 3. Prefix search: traverse characters, check if path exists. Step-by-step insertion for word 'cat': start at root, check child 'c', if not exist create node, move to 'c', then 'a', then 't', mark 't' as end. Time complexity: O(n) per operation for word length n. Space: O(n * alphabet size) but shared prefixes reduce this. Connection to L4: Compressed Trie builds on this by optimizing node storage. Common mistakes: forgetting to mark end flags, or incorrect traversal. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False. Insert: node = root; for char in word: if char not in node.children: node.children[char] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[char]; node.is_end = True.

examples.trace: 1. Root: no children. 2. For 'c': create child 'c'. 3. For 'a': at 'c', create child 'a'. 4. For 't': at 'a', create child 't'. 5. Set is_end=True at 't'. Result: chain of nodes.
1. Start at root, child 'c' exists. 2. Move to 'c', child 'a' exists. 3. Move to 'a', no child 'r'. Result: not found.

testCases.expected: Trie: d->o with children g (is_end=True) and t (is_end=True)
Mark 'p' at second level as is_end=True
Ignore or mark root as is_end (implementation-specific)

comparison.content:
### Approach Comparison for Trie Insertion

| Approach                        | Time Complexity | Space Complexity          | Best Use Case                          | Drawbacks                              |
|--------------------------------|-----------------|---------------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Array (26 children)            | O(L)           | O(L × 26) worst           | Small fixed alphabet (English letters) | Wastes memory when many letters missing|
| HashMap Children               | O(L)           | O(L)                      | Sparse tries, any character set        | Slightly slower constant factor        |
| Compressed Trie (Patricia)     | O(L)           | O(L) best case            | Very long common prefixes              | Complex implementation (split/merge)   |

**Key Takeaways:**
- Use **Array** for simplicity and speed when inputs are lowercase English letters.
- Use **HashMap** for better memory usage and flexibility.
- Use **Compressed Trie** only when space optimization is critical and you can handle extra complexity.
